summary: Jeff Atwood explores the surprisingly varied ways programmers pronounce ASCII characters when reading code aloud. Prompted by a comment using the term 'octothorpe' for the hash/pound symbol, he presents an extensive table from The New Hacker's Dictionary cataloging common and rare names for special characters. The post reveals that despite programmers' reputation for precision, there's remarkable inconsistency in how we verbalize symbols like !, #, *, and ~. Atwood highlights both practical pronunciations (bang, hash, splat) and whimsical alternatives (wax/wane for parentheses, Nathan Hale for asterisk), celebrating the quirky subculture of programmer jargon.
key_points: Programmers regularly need to read code aloud but rarely think about how inconsistent their character pronunciations are
The same symbol can have dozens of names - the # alone includes hash, pound, octothorpe, crunch, mesh, pig-pen, and tictactoe
Regional differences matter: Americans say 'pound' for # while most other cultures say 'hash'
The jargon includes both practical shorthand (bang, splat, pipe) and playful nonsense (wax/wane, embrace/bracelet, suck/blow)
Even the rigid, literal-minded programmer community embraces surprising linguistic creativity in this domain
money_quotes: How do you pronounce these unusual ASCII characters?
I'm often surprised to hear what other programmers name their ASCII characters.
There's far more variability than you'd expect considering the rigid, highly literal mindset of most programmers.
Underscores, pipes, octothorpes, curly braces – sheesh... I'll take a mild dose of verbosity if means I don't have to code something that looks like it's been zipped already!
So the next time a programmer walks up to you and says, 'oh, it's easy! Just type wax bang at hash buck grapes circumflex and splat wane,' you'll know what they mean. Maybe.
key_insight: The varied and often whimsical ways programmers pronounce ASCII characters reveals an unexpected linguistic creativity within a community known for literal precision.
